mcqs.question: What is the primary difference between Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs) and Contract Accounts (CAs)?

options.text: EOAs are controlled by private keys while CAs are controlled by smart contract code
EOAs can execute smart contracts while CAs cannot
EOAs have associated code while CAs do not
EOAs cannot hold funds while CAs can

mcqs.explanation: EOAs are controlled by private keys owned by users, while Contract Accounts are controlled by their smart contract code and have no private key.

mcqs.question: Which of these actions can an Externally Owned Account (EOA) perform?

options.text: Execute complex smart contract logic autonomously
Initiate transactions to transfer ETH or trigger smart contracts
Store and modify its own code
Create new blockchain protocols

mcqs.explanation: EOAs can initiate transactions to transfer native cryptocurrency or trigger smart contract execution, but cannot execute complex logic on their own.

mcqs.question: What happens when an EOA sends a transaction to a Contract Account?

options.text: The Contract Account's code is executed
The Contract Account sends back its private key
The EOA receives the Contract Account's storage data
Nothing happens as Contract Accounts cannot receive transactions

mcqs.explanation: When an EOA sends a transaction to a Contract Account, it triggers the execution of the smart contract's code.

mcqs.question: Which component is NOT part of an Ethereum account's anatomy?

options.text: Nonce
Balance
Private Key
Storage Hash

mcqs.explanation: While private keys control EOAs, they are not part of the account's on-chain anatomy. The four components are nonce, balance, storage hash, and code hash.

mcqs.question: Why can't Contract Accounts initiate transactions on their own?

options.text: They don't have private keys
They don't have enough computational power
They are not connected to the network
They are controlled by miners

mcqs.explanation: Contract Accounts cannot initiate transactions because they don't have private keys. They can only react to transactions sent to them.
